title: Today's AI Brief: How we used Gemini to build Google I/O 2026
summary: Google's use of Gemini for I/O 2026, and the need for better AI impact measurement
imageAlt: Image from source article for How we used Gemini to build Google I/O 2026
imageCaption: How we used Gemini to build Google I/O 2026
imageSourceTitle: How we used Gemini to build Google I/O 2026

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your AI brief. Today, I'm looking at a couple of interesting developments in the world of artificial intelligence. First, Google's revealing how they used their Gemini model to build I/O 2026. And second, a call for better measurement of AI's impact on humans. It's a lot to unpack, so let's dive in.

Google's blog post on how they used Gemini to build I/O 2026 is pretty fascinating. Apparently, Gemini helped create everything from the jellyfish pre-show to the 'TPU Training Day' film. What's interesting here is how AI can be used to streamline event production, making it more efficient and cost-effective. I mean, who doesn't love a good jellyfish animation? It's a great example of AI being used in a creative way.

Also on the board is a piece from IEEE Spectrum on why we're not measuring how AI affects humans. The article argues that while we're great at evaluating AI's technical abilities, we're not doing enough to understand its societal impact. My read is that this is a crucial gap in our understanding of AI's role in society. As AI systems become more capable, we need to start thinking about how they're affecting us, beyond just their technical prowess. It's a bit like trying to understand how a new medicine affects people, but instead of just looking at the chemistry, we're looking at the entire ecosystem.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
